title: Correspondence Theory - Sahlqvist and van Benthem

charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of standard translation, second-order obstruction, Sahlqvist formulas, and the van Benthem algorithm for computing first-order correspondents.

nodes.label:
Week 11
Correspondence Theory
nodes.note: Week 11 connects modal logic to first-order frame properties: when can a modal axiom be re-expressed as a first-order condition on the accessibility relation, and how to compute it.

nodes.label:
Modal
Applications
nodes.note: Different interpretations of box (knowledge, belief, obligation, time, programs) all need different frame classes to validate the desired axioms.

nodes.label:
Epistemic
Axioms
nodes.note: True Knowledge (Box p to p), Positive Introspection (Box p to BoxBox p), and Negative Introspection (not-Box p to Box not-Box p) characterise how knowledge or belief should behave.

nodes.label:
Standard
Translation
nodes.note: Inductive translation ST_x(F) of a modal formula into first-order logic, mapping diamond to exists and box to forall over the accessibility relation.

nodes.label: Theorem 11.2
nodes.note: Modal satisfiability coincides with first-order satisfiability via the standard translation: M, w models F iff M*, h(x)=w models ST_x(F).

nodes.label:
Second-Order
Problem
nodes.note: Frame validity quantifies over all valuations, and hence over all unary predicates. This makes the resulting formula second-order, not first-order.

nodes.label:
Chagrova's
Theorem
nodes.note: It is undecidable in general whether a given modal formula has a first-order correspondent, so no universal algorithm can exist for arbitrary modal axioms.

nodes.label:
Positive vs
Negative Formulas
nodes.note: A formula is positive in p if every occurrence of p is under an even number of negations, negative if every occurrence is under an odd number. They are monotonic in opposite directions.

nodes.label:
Lemma 11.5
(monotonicity)
nodes.note: Positive formulas are preserved when valuations grow; negative formulas are preserved when valuations shrink. This monotonicity is the key to the Sahlqvist algorithm.

nodes.label:
Sahlqvist
Formulas
nodes.note: A syntactically defined class of modal formulas built from atoms, boxed atoms, negative parts, and positive consequents. Every Sahlqvist formula has a first-order correspondent.

nodes.label: Theorem 11.8
nodes.note: Sahlqvist's Theorem: every Sahlqvist formula has a first-order correspondent, and there is an algorithm to compute it - the Sahlqvist-van Benthem algorithm.

nodes.label:
Sahlqvist-van
Benthem Algorithm
nodes.note: Five-step procedure: standard translation, pull out diamonds, group into REL/AT/BOX/NEG/POS, identify minimal valuation, substitute and simplify.

nodes.label:
Lambda
Expressions
nodes.note: Lambda notation is used to write the minimal valuation sigma(P) as a unary predicate. Application substitutes a term for the bound variable.

nodes.label:
Standard
Axiom Table
nodes.note: T = reflexivity, 4 = transitivity, B = symmetry, 5 = euclidean, D = serial, K = always valid. Each gives a first-order frame condition computed by the algorithm.

charts.title: Standard Translation ST_x(F)
charts.description: Recursive procedure that converts a modal formula into a first-order formula via the standard translation.

nodes.label:
Modal formula F
and variable x
nodes.note: We are given a modal formula F and a current world variable x. We will recursively build the first-order translation ST_x(F).

nodes.label:
Top connective
of F?
nodes.note: Identify the outermost connective of F and apply the matching translation rule.

nodes.label:
Use unary
predicate P_i(x)
nodes.note: If F = p_i, the translation is the unary predicate P_i applied to the current variable x.

nodes.label:
Negate the
translation of G
nodes.note: If F = not G, the translation is not ST_x(G). Negation passes through unchanged.

nodes.label:
Combine ST_x(G)
and ST_x(H)
nodes.note: If F is a binary Boolean connective, translate both sides separately and combine them with the same connective.

nodes.label:
exists y. R(x,y)
and ST_y(G)
nodes.note: If F = diamond G, introduce a fresh variable y, assert R(x,y), and recursively translate G at y.

nodes.label:
forall y. R(x,y)
implies ST_y(G)
nodes.note: If F = box G, introduce a fresh variable y and require that for every R-successor y the translation of G holds.

nodes.label: Output ST_x(F)
nodes.note: Combine the recursive results into the full first-order translation. By Theorem 11.2 it preserves modal satisfiability.

charts.title: Sahlqvist-van Benthem Algorithm
charts.description: Five-step procedure for computing the first-order frame correspondent of a Sahlqvist implication.

nodes.label:
Sahlqvist implication
F -> G
nodes.note: We start with a modal formula that is known to be a Sahlqvist implication. The algorithm will compute its first-order frame correspondent.

nodes.label:
Step 1: Compute
ST_x(F -> G)
nodes.note: Apply the standard translation to the entire implication, producing a first-order formula with predicate variables for each propositional variable.

nodes.label:
Step 2: Pull existentials
from antecedent
nodes.note: Move every existential quantifier in the antecedent to the front of the entire implication using the equivalence (exists y A) -> B = forall y (A -> B).

nodes.label:
Step 3: Group into
REL ^ AT ^ BOX ^ NEG -> POS
nodes.note: Identify the relational atoms (REL), atomic predicates on the current world (AT), boxed atoms (BOX), negative formulas (NEG), and the positive consequent (POS).

nodes.label:
Step 4: Read off minimal
valuation sigma(P)
nodes.note: Construct the smallest unary predicate that makes AT ^ BOX trivially true. Express it as a lambda expression in terms of the existing variables.

nodes.label:
Step 5: Substitute sigma(P)
and simplify
nodes.note: Replace every occurrence of P in ST_x(F->G) with sigma(P), beta-reduce, and simplify. The resulting formula contains no predicate variables.

nodes.label:
More propositional
variables remain?
nodes.note: If the original implication has multiple propositional variables, repeat steps 3-5 for each variable in turn until none remain.

nodes.label:
First-order
correspondent
nodes.note: The simplified universally-quantified formula is the first-order correspondent of the Sahlqvist implication, characterising the frame property it captures.

charts.title: Identifying a Sahlqvist Formula
charts.description: Decision procedure for determining whether a candidate modal formula is a Sahlqvist formula and therefore amenable to the algorithm.

nodes.label: Modal formula F
nodes.note: We have a modal formula and want to decide whether it is a Sahlqvist formula and therefore guaranteed to have a computable first-order correspondent.

nodes.label:
F is an
implication?
nodes.note: Check whether F has the form A -> B at its top level. Sahlqvist implications must have this shape (possibly within the scope of box or wedge of variable-disjoint parts).

nodes.label:
Not a Sahlqvist
formula
nodes.note: The candidate fails one of the structural conditions, so the Sahlqvist-van Benthem algorithm does not apply directly. By Chagrova's theorem, the existence of a correspondent is undecidable in general.
Check that the antecedent is a Sahlqvist antecedent: built using only conjunction and disjunction from atoms, boxed atoms, top, bottom, and negative formulas.

nodes.label:
Consequent is
positive?
nodes.note: Check that the consequent is a positive formula: every propositional variable occurs under an even number of negations.

nodes.label:
Sahlqvist
implication
nodes.note: The implication itself is a Sahlqvist implication. It can be processed directly by the Sahlqvist-van Benthem algorithm.

nodes.label:
Apply Sahlqvist-van
Benthem algorithm
nodes.note: By Theorem 11.8 the formula has a first-order correspondent, and the Sahlqvist-van Benthem algorithm computes it explicitly.

charts.title: Common Modal Axioms and Frame Correspondents
charts.description: Taxonomy of standard modal axioms together with their corresponding first-order frame properties.

nodes.label:
Standard
Axioms
nodes.note: Each named axiom corresponds to a structural property of the accessibility relation R, computable by the Sahlqvist-van Benthem algorithm.

nodes.label: T: Box p to p
nodes.note: True knowledge axiom. Saying that knowledge implies truth corresponds to the relation being reflexive.

nodes.label:
Reflexive
forall x. R(x,x)
nodes.note: Every world has an edge to itself. This is the simplest frame property and characterises modal logic KT.

nodes.label: 4: Box p to BoxBox p
nodes.note: Positive introspection axiom. If we know p, we know that we know p. Corresponds to transitivity of R.

nodes.label:
Transitive
R(x,y) ^ R(y,z) -> R(x,z)
nodes.note: If x sees y and y sees z, then x sees z. Characterises modal logic K4.

nodes.label: B: p to BoxDiamond p
nodes.note: Brouwer-style axiom. If p is true here then p is necessarily possible. Corresponds to symmetry.

nodes.label:
Symmetric
R(x,y) -> R(y,x)
nodes.note: Every relation pair is mirrored. Symmetry by itself characterises the modal logic KB.

nodes.label: 5: Diamond p to BoxDiamond p
nodes.note: Negative introspection axiom (in disguise). Whatever is possible is necessarily possible. Corresponds to the Euclidean property.

nodes.label:
Euclidean
R(x,y) ^ R(x,z) -> R(y,z)
nodes.note: Any two successors of a world are themselves connected. Characterises the modal logic K5.

nodes.label: D: Box p to Diamond p
nodes.note: Consistency axiom: necessary p implies possible p. Corresponds to seriality.

nodes.label:
Serial
forall x. exists y. R(x,y)
nodes.note: Every world has at least one successor, so the agent always considers something possible.

nodes.label: K: Box(p->q) -> (Box p -> Box q)
nodes.note: The K axiom is valid in every Kripke frame, so its first-order correspondent is just top.
